task:
[Instruction] You are given an excerpt from a script: 
 (opening:     The camera slowly zooms through a variety of insects and rocks. We follow a small yellow bug climb up a blade of grass.  As it spreads its wings to fly  we are whisked along the Australian outback and prairie by Ayers rock and eventually slow down as we approach Cody's house.)

(scene:  inside Cody's room.  The camera pans around to show Cody sleeping in his hammock.  The sound of Faloo's call is heard.  Cody hears it  jumps out of bed  and runs to the window.  He puts on his shirt and grabs his knife.)

(scene:  Cody sneaks past his mother who is in the kitchen listening to the radio.)

Announcer:    ... thundershowers are expected in the Crocodile Falls area and some of the surrounding gullies so take out your...

(scene:  Outside Cody's house.  Cody leaves the house  and closes the door behind him  but not quietly.)

Mom:     (from inside upon hearing the door) Cody!

Cody:    (whincing) Yeah mom?

Mom:     What about your breakfast?

Cody:    I've got some sandwiches in my pack.

Mom:     Well be home for supper.

Cody:    (hopping the gate) No worries mom.

(scene:  Cody runs toward the forest; Faloo's call is heard in the background.  He runs past some rock formations and enters the woods.  Birds follow him; and squak at him.)

Cody:    (to the birds) I know  I'm coming.
 (Cody jumps over a hollow log) Hustle up Nelson  Faloo's sounding the call!
 (Cody slides through a log  picks up a stick  and beats on the roof of the wombats home.) C'mon little wombats  hurry!
 (Cody continues to run through the forest with all of the animals following him.)
 (Cody arrives at the tree where Faloo has been sounding the call.)
 (to Faloo) Who's caught this time?

Faloo:   You don't know her  Cody  her name is Marahute  the great golden eagle.

Cody:    Where is she?

Faloo:   She's caught  high on a cliff in a poacher's trap.  You're the only one who can reach her.

Cody:    I'll get her loose.

Faloo:   Right-oh  hop on  no time to lose.
 (Cody hops onto Faloo and they travel through the forest and along a stream/river; more scenes of animals and the forest.)
 (They arrive at the cliff.)
 (pointing up towards the cliff) She's up on top of that ridge.  Be careful lit'l friend.

(scene:  various "time lapse" views of Cody climbing up the cliff.)
 (Cody reaches the top and sees the eagle.)

Cody:    Marahute!
 (Cody looks at the eagle; he approaches her slowly; she hears him and wakes up; Marahute screeches and struggles to get free.)
 (reassuring) Calm down  calm down.  I'm not gonna hurt you.  (Cody strokes Marahute on the head) That's a girl. Stay still... it's o.k.
 (Cody gets out his knife; Marahute sees the glint of the knife and    begins to struggle and scream)
 No wait!  I'm here to help you... easy!... easy!
 (Cody cuts two ropes.  Cody cuts the last rope to free Marahute.)
 You're free!!
 (As Marahute spreads her wings to fly  she knocks Cody off the cliff.)
 Aaaiigh!
 (Cody falls; Marahute dives down to catch him; she catches him just before he hits the ground; they begin to fly around; the animals see Cody on Marahute and stand in awe; Marahute files over several rock formations; the fly up above the clouds; Cody looks at his reflection in Marahute's eye.)
 Higher!
 (They fly even higher above the clouds; Marahute throws Cody and catches him; Cody is now held in Marahute's talons.)
 Woah!
 (Cody mocks an eagle screech; he laughs as Marahute tickles him; they cruise above the clouds which eventually open up to show the ground; Marahute nose dives towards the ground and a stream; she holds Cody just high enough above the water so that he is water skiing; they approach a flock of birds; Marahute lets Cody go and he skims through the birds  scattering them; Marahute grabs Cody just before he falls in and then put Cody right in front of her  on her beak (pushing him from behind); they go over the egde of a waterfall; Marahute catches Cody again; this time he rides by standing on her back; they arrive at Marahute's nest)
 Wow!
 (Cody and Marahute look at each other; Cody falls over as he attempts to look at Marahute upside down. Marahute moves some grass and feathers to show Cody her eggs)
 You're a mom!
 (Cody puts his ear to the eggs)
 They're very warm.  Are they gonna hatch soon?
 (Marahute ruffles her neck feathers in an affectionate manner; she sits on the eggs and then looks out "over her domain".)
 Where's the daddy eagle?  (Marahute drops her head) Oh... my dad's gone too.
 (Cody give Marahute an affectionate stroke;  as they fix the covering on the eggs  the wind picks up and blows a feather in Cody's face; he looks at it  plays with it  and puts it back.  Marahute picks it up and gives it to Cody and he gives her a hug.)
 (Marahute and Cody are now on the ground; Marahute takes off and Cody runs around making flying noises)

(scene:  just inside the forest.  A wanted poster of McLeach is posted on a tree; A mouse is tied up with a bell attached to it that rings as it struggles; Cody hears the bell and goes over to the mouse.)

Cody:    Heh heh... hey little fella  what happened to you?

Baitmouse:    (panicking) Oh no! No  no  no  no!!  Get away  get away! It's a trap  it's a trap.  Be careful  NO!

Cody:    (as the mouse is speaking) Don't worry  I'll get you loose.  Woah! (Cody falls into the trap.  He looks up to see a blinking light. 

[Requirement] Based on this, continue the scene and develop the storyline. The continuation must be {word_count_type} {word_count} words long.

response:
Cody lands hard on a bed of dry leaves and sticks inside a pit that’s been camouflaged with lattice and brush.  A single battery-powered beacon blinks red, ticking like a metronome.  Dust swirls; the air smells of kerosene and damp earth.  He scrambles up the slick clay walls, fingers clawing, but every handhold crumbles.  The rim is two body-lengths above his head.

A shadow eclipses the opening.  A steel hook clangs against rock; a rope snakes down and whips around Cody’s waist before he can shout.  With a jerk he’s yanked upward, boots scraping dirt, and dumped onto the leaf litter.  The man holding the rope is seven feet of sun-bleached denim and crocodile scars.  A broad-brimmed hat hides his eyes, but the voice is syrup over broken glass.

McLeach:  “Well, well.  The little bush telegraph himself.  I set that snare for a gold-feathered hen and caught myself a chick instead.”

Cody rolls, trying to free the knife at his belt, but McLeach stamps a boot on his wrist.  The blade skitters away.  A joey-sized sack is produced, burlap reeking of fish oil, and cinched over Cody’s head.  Darkness.  The world tilts as he’s slung across a shoulder like a hunted roo.

Sound of boots, then the creak of a rusted truck door.  Cody is thrown onto corrugated-iron flooring; the sack loosens just enough for him to glimpse the cage welded into the tray.  Inside, a sulphur-crested cockatoo with one wing crudely clipped hops and hisses.

Frank:  “New cellmate, eh?  Welcome to the Ritz.”

The truck lurches.  Gravel pings the panels.  Cody’s stomach sloshes as they bounce downhill.  He wriggles until the sack slips off.  The cockatoo eyes him, head tilted like a guilty conscience.

Frank (low):  “Name’s Frank.  Used to fly courier for the ranger until McLeach clipped my ticket.  You?”

Cody:  “Cody.  I’ve got to get back—there’s an eagle in trouble.”

Frank:  “Golden girl named Marahute?  McLeach has been singing about her since dawn.  Wants her eggs for the black market.  You’re the bait, kid.”

Cody’s blood ices.  He studies the cage: rebar grid, padlock the size of a mango, riveted to the truck chassis.  Outside, eucalyptus blur into red dust.  He wedges fingers through the bars, feeling for weakness; there is none.

Frank:  “Save your nails.  We need the key.  It’s on his belt next to the remote detonator for the net cannon.  Real sweetheart, our host.”

The truck slows, engine idling.  Voices.  McLeach is talking to someone—no, to a radio.  Static crackles.

Radio:  “…repeat, ranger aircraft grid-searching sector nine, over.”

McLeach laughs:  “Tell ’em I’m fishing at Crocodile Falls, right where the map says.  They’ll waste a week.”

Truck rolls again.  Cody closes his eyes, picturing Marahute’s nest, the three cream-coloured eggs nestled in down.  He whispers:  “Hold on.”

Night falls like a slammed hatch.  The truck stops; the engine ticks.  McLeach’s boots crunch away; a campfire blooms orange through the canopy.  Smell of charred goanna.  Cody waits until snores rumble, then nudges Frank.

Cody:  “Can you pick a lock?”

Frank:  “Wings clipped, not brains.  But I need something thin and rigid.”

Cody scans the cage floor.  A twist of fencing wire glints under moonlight.  He pries it free, straightens it against his teeth, passes it through the bars.  Frank’s beak works like needle-nose pliers, feeding the wire into the padlock.  Click—soft as a heartbeat.  The door swings.

They freeze.  McLeach snorts, rolls over on his swag, hat brim flopping.  Firelight reveals the key ring clipped to his belt beside a sheathed hunting knife.  Cody eases out, bare feet on cool sand.  Each step is a held breath.  He crouches, fingers trembling, unhooks the ring.  A metal jingle—too loud.  McLeach’s eyes snap open, mirror-bright.

McLeach:  “Going somewhere, boy?”

Cody bolts.  The key ring flies, scattering in the dust.  McLeach lunges, hat spinning off, revealing a face mapped by sun and hate.  Cody dives under the truck, emerges the other side, sprinting toward the tree line.  A spotlight floods the dark; the truck’s roof rack battery beam pins him.  McLeach’s shotgun racks.

McLeach:  “I can wing a galah at fifty paces, kid.  Don’t make me ruin the merchandise.”

Cody stops, chest heaving.  Behind him, a rustle—Frank limps into the light, dragging the padlock in his beak.  He drops it, clang, and screeches full volume.  The night explodes with cockatoo thunder; roosting corellas rise in a white cyclone, shrieking.  McLeach flinches, swatting air.  Cody dashes again, but a root snags his ankle; he sprawls.  The shotgun barrels hover inches from his nose.

McLeach:  “Enough bedtime stories.”

A new sound—high, keening, avian.  From the star-field above, a silhouette eclipses the moon: wings eight feet wide, talons curled.  Marahute stoops like a golden meteor.  McLeach swings the gun up; too late.  Talons rake his shoulder, spinning him.  The shotgun roars, pellets shredding bark.  Marahute banks, screaming defiance.

Cody scrambles to his feet, arms waving.

Cody:  “Marahute, the eggs!  He wants the eggs!”

The eagle’s eyes ignite.  She dives again; McLeach drops, rolls, grabs Cody by the collar, presses the muzzle under the boy’s ear.

McLeach:  “Call her off or the kid’s a memory.”

Marahute hovers, wings beating slow thunder, dust swirling like bronze snow.  Her gaze meets Cody’s—ancient, fierce, tender.  Cody’s hand inches to his pocket; he still has the feather she gave him.  He holds it up; moonlight turns it to flame.  A promise.

Cody (soft):  “Trust me.”

He jams the feather into the shotgun’s ejection port.  McLeach pulls the trigger; the weapon jams, breech locked.  In that heartbeat Marahute strikes—not with talons but with wind.  Her wings buffet, knocking McLeach backward into the campfire.  Sparks fountain; he howls, beating at embers on his shirt.  Cody sprints to the truck, snatches the net-cannon remote from the dashboard, and hurls it into the dark.  Frank flaps up beside him.

Frank:  “Highway’s that way, two clicks.  Ranger station another ten.  Your call.”

Cody looks at Marahute circling overhead, then toward the distant cliffs where her nest waits.  He cups hands to mouth.

Cody:  “I’ll lead him away from the eggs!  Meet me at the ranger’s!”

Marahute screams protest, but Cody is already running, Frank on his shoulder like a feathered conscience.  Behind them, McLeach kicks free of the fire, shotgun discarded, grabbing a high-powered rifle from the cab.  His voice rips the night.

McLeach:  “You can run, boy, but the desert’s mine!”

Cody and Frank vanish into scrub, moon silvering their path.  Far above, Marahute climbs, a gold speck against the Milky Way, guarding the horizon where three unhatched lives sleep under starlight.  The chase has begun, and the Outback itself seems to hold its breath, listening for the next footfall.

task:
[Instruction] You are given a summary of an article: 
 Taraji P. Henson revealed her son was 'racially profiled' by police officers .
Claims police stopped her son Marcel at University of Southern California .
USC's Public Safety boss says he has experienced racial profiling himself .
John Thomas has vowed to carry out his own investigation into the matter  . 

[Requirement] Write an article based on this summary. The article must be {word_count_type} {word_count} words long.
raw_data:
Taraji P. Henson revealed her son was 'racially profiled' by police officers .
Claims police stopped her son Marcel at University of Southern California .
USC's Public Safety boss says he has experienced racial profiling himself .
John Thomas has vowed to carry out his own investigation into the matter .

Taraji P. Henson Says Her Son Was Racially Profiled by Campus Police: A Mother’s Cry for Justice Echoes Across USC

Los Angeles, CA — When Academy-Award-nominated actress Taraji P. Henson stepped up to the podium at a hastily arranged press conference on the University of Southern California campus Tuesday afternoon, her voice cracked only once. Clad in a simple black blazer and jeans, the star of “Empire” and “Hidden Figures” clutched a single sheet of paper that trembled slightly in her manicured fingers. The paper contained the details of an incident she says has forever altered the way her 20-year-old son, Marcel Johnson, views the world—and the way she views the university he once dreamed of attending.

“Marcel was not stopped because he ran a red light,” Henson began, her eyes scanning the rows of reporters, students, and faculty who had gathered beneath the towering fig trees outside Bovard Auditorium. “He was not stopped because his tags were expired. He was stopped because he is a young Black man driving a nice car in a neighborhood that some people still think he doesn’t belong in.”

According to Henson, the encounter occurred on the evening of October 3, just after 8:15 p.m., as Marcel was leaving an off-campus study group near Exposition Park. Driving the 2022 Lexus SUV his mother bought him as a reward for maintaining a 3.7 GPA, Marcel was allegedly pulled over by two USC Department of Public Safety (DPS) officers who, Henson claims, never articulated a clear reason for the stop. The actress says her son was ordered out of the vehicle, frisked, and questioned for nearly 25 minutes while officers ran his license and registration multiple times. No citation was issued. No warning was given. The officers simply returned his documents and drove away.

“He called me crying,” Henson recalled, her voice dropping to a whisper. “Not the kind of crying you do when you’re sad. The kind of crying you do when you’re scared, when you realize your life can be taken from you for nothing.”

The incident might have gone unnoticed had Henson not taken to Instagram the following morning, posting a tearful video that has since been viewed more than 4.2 million times. In the clip, she tagged USC President Carol Folt and the university’s public safety department, demanding transparency and accountability. Within hours, #JusticeForMarcel was trending nationwide, and students had begun organizing a sit-in at the campus’s iconic Tommy Trojan statue.

By Tuesday afternoon, USC’s highest-ranking public safety official, Chief John Thomas, agreed to meet with Henson and her legal team. In an unexpected twist, Thomas—who is Black—opened the meeting by sharing his own experience of being racially profiled by police in his native Atlanta two decades ago. “I know what it feels like to be stopped for ‘driving while Black,’” Thomas told reporters after the 90-minute closed-door session. “It is a humiliation that never leaves you. I promised Ms. Henson that I will personally oversee an internal investigation, and I will not hesitate to recommend policy changes, retraining, or disciplinary action if our review finds misconduct.”

The university, for its part, released a carefully worded statement expressing “deep concern” and pledging “a thorough, impartial, and expedited review.” But for many students, faculty, and alumni, the statement felt insufficient. Dr. Shaun Harper, executive director of the USC Race and Equity Center, pointed to a 2019 campus climate survey in which 68 percent of Black male students reported feeling unfairly targeted by DPS. “This is not an isolated incident,” Harper said. “It is the latest eruption of a long-smoldering problem.”

Across campus, reactions ranged from outrage to exhaustion. Junior Maya Delgado, a political science major, held a cardboard sign that read, “If Taraji’s son isn’t safe, none of us are.” Delgado, who identifies as Afro-Latina, said she has been stopped three times since August for what officers called “routine safety checks.” “They ask for my ID even when I’m walking,” she said. “They never ask my white roommates.”

For Henson, the fight is both public and deeply personal. Raised in a working-class neighborhood in southeast Washington, D.C., she has often spoken about the anxiety Black mothers carry every time their children leave the house. “I thought success would protect him,” she said, referencing her Emmy- and Oscar-nominated career. “I thought if I gave him a good car, good clothes, good grades—if I taught him to speak perfect English and keep his hands on the steering wheel at 10 and 2—he would be safe. But success doesn’t shield you from racism. It just makes people resent you more.”

The actress has retained civil rights attorney Benjamin Crump, who has represented the families of Trayvon Martin, Michael Brown, and George Floyd. Crump says he will request body-camera footage, dashboard-camera footage, and radio communications from the night in question. Under California’s new Racial and Identity Profiling Act, law enforcement agencies must collect and publish detailed stop data, including perceived race and the stated reason for each stop. Crump intends to subpoena three years of DPS records to determine whether Black drivers are disproportionately targeted. “We are prepared to file a federal civil rights lawsuit if necessary,” Crump warned. “USC has a choice: be a leader in equity or a defendant in court.”

The stakes are high for the university, which is still recovering from scandals involving former campus gynecologist George Tyndall and the Varsity Blues admissions bribery case. Enrollment of Black students has declined 22 percent since 2015, according to university data, and donors have begun to express concern. One prominent alumnus, who asked not to be named, told the Los Angeles Times that he has paused a $5 million pledge until he sees “concrete evidence that USC is serious about racial justice.”

Meanwhile, Marcel Johnson has decided to finish the semester remotely. “I love my school,” he said in a brief interview outside the campus bookstore, his mother’s hand resting protectively on his shoulder. “I love my professors, my friends, the late-night study sessions. But every time I see a DPS cruiser, my heart races. I don’t know when—or if—that will go away.”

Chief Thomas says he hopes to complete the internal investigation within 45 days and has invited the Los Angeles County Sheriff’s Department to conduct an independent parallel review. He has also pledged to implement a pilot program that would require officers to provide drivers with a business card containing their name, badge number, and the specific reason for every stop. “Transparency is the first step toward trust,” Thomas insisted.

Whether those measures will satisfy Henson remains to be seen. Standing beneath the golden light of a California sunset, she offered a quiet but unflinching warning: “I am not going anywhere. I will be at every board meeting, every town hall, every rally until USC proves that my son—and every other Black child—can pursue an education without being treated like a suspect.”

As the crowd dispersed, a group of students began to chant, “Say his name—Marcel Johnson.” The sound echoed across the brick-lined walkways, past the flowering jacarandas, and up toward the administrative towers where university officials now face a defining choice. In the age of viral videos and racial reckoning, silence is no longer an option. For Taraji P. Henson, for her son, and for thousands of students who say they have lived this story in smaller, quieter ways, the next 45 days will determine whether USC can transform outrage into institutional change—or whether another mother’s tears will simply dissolve into the Pacific dusk.
